segments.s616-g113: چه داند is a rhetorical interrogative: چه (here ‘how/what’ in the sense ‘how could’) + داند (3rd person singular present of دانستن, ‘he knows’), so ‘how would he know’. که introduces the subordinate clause. مردم کدامست is an inverted order of ‘کدامِ مردم است’ (کدامست = archaic contraction of کدام است, ‘which is’), i.e. ‘which people it is/which of the people’. The final به is a short prepositional/relational particle (used poetically) meaning roughly ‘about/with regard to’ or linking this clause to what follows; word order is poetic and elements are frequently inverted.
segments.s616-g114: چگونه شناسد begins a new question: چگونه (‘how’) + شناسد (3rd person singular present of شناختن/شناسیدن, here ‘he recognizes/knows’ — note the archaic -د ending). کهان را is the definite/direct object (کهان ‘the world’/‘the heavens’) marked by را. ز مه uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) ‘from’ + مه ‘the moon’. Literally: ‘How can he recognise the world from the moon?’ — i.e. how can he distinguish the world from the moon.
segments.s616-g65: کنون (now) is a temporal adverb placed at the start to set the moment. آرزوها is the direct object (plural) 'wishes'; بیاریم is a first-person-plural subjunctive/optative form meaning 'let us bring' or 'we bring' (classical/poetic usage of ب + آوردن), linking the speaker to the action; گرم here modifies آرزوها and means 'ardent' or 'fervent' rather than literal temperature. Word order follows classical Persian: temporal adverb – object – verb phrase with adjective after the object.
segments.s616-g66: هم از آغازِ این عبارت means 'also/from among'—هم adds the sense 'also' or 'besides'. از introduces the source or partitive 'from/of'. چندگونه is an adjective meaning 'various' that governs خورشهای (plural construct). خورشهای is the plural of خورش 'a cooked dish/stew'; نرم qualifies those dishes as 'tender/soft'. The phrase lists additional items taken from several types of soft dishes.
segments.s616-g67: بگفت is a past narrative verb 'he said' (classical compound form). این refers to 'this' (the foregoing statement). و here simply connects clauses 'and/then'. زان پس is a temporal/causal phrase 'after that' or 'thereupon'. به بازار شد is an idiomatic past expression meaning 'he went to the bazaar' or 'he came to the market', with شد used as the narrative past auxiliary.
segments.s616-g68: به ساز دگرگون is an adverbial phrase literally 'to/with a changed tune' (ساز = tune/melody; دگرگون = changed), idiomatically indicating a change of mood or manner. خریدار شد is a simple past 'he became a purchaser' (خریدار = buyer; شد = became). Together the clause says his attitude/mode changed and he took on the role of buyer.
segments.s616-g69: This is a simple list of food items with a brief verb: شکر (sugar) followed by جست, an archaic/past form of جستن used here to mean 'took/selected' in narrative poetry. The repeated و link the list items بادام (almonds), مرغ (poultry/fowl) and بره (lamb). Word order is verb after the first noun, a common poetic compression.
segments.s616-g70: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. آرایش خوان is a genitive-like compound where آرایش ('arrangement/ornament') qualifies خوان ('the banquet/table'); کند is the present/past classical form 'does/makes' used here as a habitual/classical present meaning 'will make' or 'makes'; یکسره qualifies the verb phrase as 'completely/entirely'. The clause describes the purpose/result of taking those foods: they complete the banquet's arrangement.
segments.s616-g71: This is another list of scented/culinary items. The surface form می‌و likely stands for 'می' (wine) or 'می‌و' (fruit/wine) in poetic diction; زعفران is 'saffron'. برد here is the past verb 'took/carried' (classical usage) followed by the conjunction و linking مشک (musk) and گلاب (rosewater). The sequence names what he took for the banquet or household.
segments.s616-g72: سوی خانه شد is a motion phrase: سوی + noun = 'toward the house', and شد used narratively as 'he went/turned'. با introduces an accompanying circumstance 'with'; دلی here is a substantive adjective 'a heart' used adverbially, and پرشتاب is an adjective 'hasty/rapid' describing that heart. The whole means he headed home with a heart full of haste.
segments.s616-g57: بشد (classical/past narrative) introduces the action and here means ‘it happened’/‘he came’ — functionally ‘the king rose/appeared’. The sequence شاه و بنشست is subject then verb: ‘the king and (then) he sat down’, with بنشست (past) ‘sat down’ and the prepositional phrase بر تخت اوی ‘upon his throne’ (بر + تخت, then the possessive اوی after the noun).
segments.s616-g58: شگفتی فروماند is a verbless/noun-verb cluster: شگفتی ‘amazement, wonder’ is the affected state and فروماند (past) indicates it remained/was left or was abated; از بخت اوی is a prepositional phrase ‘because of / regarding his fate’ where بخت is ‘fate/destiny’ and اوی is the possessive ‘his’. The overall sense: ‘astonishment (with respect) to his fate remained.’
segments.s616-g59: جوان رفت و آورد is a two-verb sequence in past tense: ‘the youth went and (then) brought’. Persian often strings actions with و between verbs to indicate successive actions. خایه دویست (object then numeral here) means ‘two hundred testicles’ — object follows the verb cluster in this poetic order.
segments.s616-g60: به استاد گفت = ‘he said to the master’; the indirect-object preposition به precedes استاد. The quoted address follows: ای گرامی is a vocative ‘O honored one’, and مه‌ایست is a nominal predicate ‘it is the moon’ (مه + است fused as poetic mah‑ist). The subject (he/the youth) is omitted as expected after گفت.
segments.s616-g61: یکی مرغ بریان با نان گرم lists an item: numeric determiner + noun + postpositive adjective (مرغ بریان ‘a roasted bird’ — in Persian the adjective commonly follows the noun), then the accompaniment phrase با نان گرم ‘with warm bread’ (با + noun + adjective).
segments.s616-g62: نبید کهن آر is an imperative/command list: نبید (wine) + کهن (aged/old) followed by آر, an archaic/poetic imperative meaning ‘bring’ (cf. classical آر for آوردن). The second half و بادام نرم continues the list: ‘and soft almonds.’
segments.s616-g63: بشد نزد بهرام گفت = ‘he came/approached and, in the presence of Bahram, said…’. نزد + name means ‘before/in the presence of’. گفت is the reporting verb ‘said’; ای سوار is the vocative ‘O rider’ addressing Bahram.
segments.s616-g64: همی خایه کردی تو دی خواستار is a compressed colloquial/poetic question/insult: همی ‘still/ever’, خایه کردی ‘you did (with) testicles’ (کردی = you did/caused; object خایه), with the pronoun تو postverbal for emphasis. دی خواستار = ‘formerly/previously a petitioner/desirer’ (دی = formerly). Together: ‘Have you long been one who seeks testicles?’ — note the archaic word order and vocative/emphatic placement of تو after the verb.
segments.s616-g17: نیاورد is a past negative verb placed before its subject (poetic inversion): “did not bring.” بازارگان is the subject ‘the merchant’ following the verb. آنچ گفت forms a relative description ‘that which (he) said’ — here آنچ = “that which” and گفت stands for the utterance referred to (literal phrasing in the manuscript puts the word for ‘said/what was said’ as a noun/phrase).
segments.s616-g18: نبد is an archaic/existential negative meaning ‘there was not / did not exist.’ مغز بادامش = ‘the kernel (مغز) of its almond (بادام + possessive -ش). اندر is the preposition ‘in/within,’ and نهفت is a past participial/poetic form meaning ‘hidden/concealed,’ so the clause says there was no almond‑kernel hidden within it.
segments.s616-g19: چو introduces a temporal clause here, meaning ‘when.’ تاریک شد is a simple past copular construction ‘became dark’ (شد = became). میزبان is the subject (‘the host’). رفت نرم places the verb رفت ‘went/left’ before the adverb نرم ‘gently/softly’ — poetic word order where the manner adverb follows the verb.
segments.s616-g20: یکی here is an indefinite ‘a certain’ / ‘one’ functioning like English ‘a’; مرغ بریان is a noun phrase ‘a roasted fowl’ (مرغ = fowl, بریان = roasted). بیاورد is a past narrative verb ‘brought’ (he went and brought), and گرم placed after the verb qualifies the brought bird as ‘hot/warm.’
segments.s616-g21: بیاراست is a past verb ‘adorned/arranged’ (here ‘set in order’). خوان is the direct object ‘the banquet/meal.’ پیش بهرام is a prepositional phrase ‘before Bahram’ indicating the recipient/location. برد is a past verb meaning ‘took/brought’ (poetic past narrative), so the clause says he arranged the feast and brought it before Bahram.
segments.s616-g22: به بازارگان گفت is the speech frame ‘he said to the merchant’ (به + indirect object + گفت). The following two words — بهرام گرد — are the words spoken; گرد here is verbal/imperative in sense ‘act / be / go about’ and the phrase is elliptical/condensed (literary), roughly ‘(be) like Bahram’ or ‘act as Bahram does’ — the exact force is slightly ambiguous in the poem but functions as the reported utterance.
segments.s616-g23: که here introduces the content/reason clause ‘that / because.’ از تو = ‘from you’ (agent/source). نبید کهن is a noun phrase ‘old wine’ (نبید = wine, کهن = old). خواستم is first‑person past ‘I wanted / I asked (for).’ Together: ‘that I asked you for old wine.’
segments.s616-g24: زبان را = ‘the tongue / my speech’ with the definite object marker را. به خواهش = ‘with entreaty / by means of a request’ (instrumental sense). بیاراستم is first‑person past ‘I adorned / I fashioned,’ so the clause means ‘I adorned my speech with entreaty’ (i.e., I phrased my words as a plea).
segments.s616-g97: بیارند is a poetic/past-narrative form (bi-ārand) meaning 'they brought' or 'they caused to come' (here used as the narrative verb). شاگرد is the noun 'pupil/apprentice' (direct object). با او بهم is a prepositional phrase: با او 'with him' and بهم 'together' (بهم emphasizes togetherness rather than disorder). Word order is verb + object + prepositional phrase, typical in Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s616-g98: یکی ... و دیگر is a simple coordination: 'one ... and the other ...'. شاد is an adjective 'happy/merry' modifying یکی. ازیشان (از ایشان) means 'of them' or 'from among them' and marks that یکی is one of the group. دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim' modifying دیگر.
segments.s616-g99: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شاگرد و استاد is the coordinated subject 'the pupil and the master'. رفتند is the third-person plural past 'they went/left'. زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying رفتند; the adverb follows the verb for emphasis in this poetic register.
segments.s616-g100: به پیش is the prepositional phrase 'to the presence of / before'. شهنشاه ایران is the title 'the Shahanshah of Iran' (noun phrase in the object of the preposition). چو دود is a simile 'like smoke' that comments on the manner of their coming—چو 'like' + دود 'smoke'.
segments.s616-g101: چو is 'when'. شاگرد را uses را as the definite/object marker to mark the pupil as the direct object. دید is the past 'saw'. بنواختش is composed of the past stem نوخت (to strike, to play) with the prefix بـ and the enclitic -ش ('him'); here it means 'he struck/played for him' (context of musician-master suggests 'played for him' or 'struck a note in his honor').
segments.s616-g102: بر plus مهتران indicates position 'before/among the chiefs/elders' (بر = 'before/upon', مهتران = 'great ones/chiefs'). شاد is an adverb/adjunct 'joyful/happily'. بنشاختش is a past-form verb with enclitic -ش; here it functions as 'he set him (to sit)/placed him' (i.e., he had him seated among the chiefs).
segments.s616-g103: یکی is the indefinite 'one' or 'a certain (one)'. بدره is a poetic noun meaning 'the full moon' but here used as a metaphor for a beautiful person (a 'beauty' brought before someone). بردند is the past 'they brought/carried'. نزدیک اوی means 'near him/her' (نزدیک = 'near', اوی = 'to him/her'); the phrase means they brought that beauty close to him.
segments.s616-g104: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/for when'. چون ماه شد means 'when she/it became like the moon' (چون = 'like/as', ماه = 'the moon', شد = 'became'). جان تاریک اوی literally 'his/her soul dark' — جان 'soul/life' + تاریک 'dark' + اوی pronoun; poetically it says his soul was overshadowed/darkened (an affective result of seeing the moon-like beauty).
segments.s616-g73: بیاورد is a third‑person narrative verb (literary past / historic present) meaning 'brought' or 'brought in' and appears before its object in VSO order. خوان (the banquet/table) is the direct object, followed by the prepositional phrase با خورش‌های نغز ('with delightful dishes'); نغز is a post‑nominal adjective modifying خورش‌ها. The whole phrase reads 'he brought a (feast) table with delightful dishes.'
segments.s616-g74: جوان is the subject 'the youth/young man.' The construction جوان بر منش بود uses بر + noun to mark the quality/condition (literary usage: 'in respect of character'), and بود is the past copula 'was.' پاکیزه‌مغز is a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted / clean‑kernelled' linked by و to form a coordinated predicate: 'he was of good character and pure‑hearted.' Word order is Subject + (prepositional quality) + copula + and + adjective.
segments.s616-g75: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when.' نان خورده شد is a perfect/passive‑style clause 'the bread had been eaten' (خورده شد with شد as auxiliary). جام پر is a noun + adjective 'the full cup.' می‌ببرد is the iterative/habitual present morphology (mi‑ prefix) used in narrative to mean 'would take/bring' or 'used to carry,' so the clause reads 'when the bread was eaten, he would take/bring the full cup.'
segments.s616-g76: نخستنی functions adverbially as 'first' or 'first of all.' به + بهرام خسرو is the recipient phrase 'to Bahram Khosrow' (name + title/appellation). سپرد is the past verb 'handed over / entrusted.' Word order is adverb (first) + indirect object + verb: 'first he handed [it] to Bahram Khosrow.'
segments.s616-g77: بدین‌گونه is an adverb meaning 'thus / in this way.' تا here introduces the result ('so that / until') and connects to the result clause شاد و خرم شدند, where شدند is the past plural copular/inchoative 'they became' and شاد و خرم are coordinated adjectives 'merry and joyful.' The sequence gives a result: 'thus, so that they became merry and joyful.'
segments.s616-g78: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' marking cause. خردک ('little piece' / morsel) names the small bits (likely of bread); به جام is the directional phrase 'to the cup.' دمادم is an adverb 'constantly / again and again.' شدند (past plural) here reports the resulting habitual state/action 'they became / kept coming'—so the clause means 'because of the little morsels, they constantly went to the cup.'
segments.s616-g79: چنین is an adverb 'thus / so.' گفت here is the past reporting verb 'said' (literary narrative past) and is followed by the prepositional phrase با میزبان شهریار — 'to/with the host, the king' where شهریار is appositive or identifies the host as 'the king.' The clause functions as the main reporting clause: 'thus the king said to the host.'
segments.s616-g80: که introduces reported content ('that'). بهرام is the person named; ما + را is the object 'us' with the definite/object marker. کند is the present/habitual form of the verb 'do/make' used here as a copular/auxiliary in combination with خواستار (an adjective 'desirous') — together 'has become desirous of' or simply 'desires.' So the subordinate clause means 'that Bahram desires us.'
segments.s616-g49: تو مهمان من باش = imperative clause telling the addressee to adopt the role 'my guest'. تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun (you); مهمان من is a possessive phrase 'my guest' (من here marks possession); باش is the imperative 'be'. با این سوار is a prepositional accompaniment 'with this rider' (با + demonstrative این + نoun سوار).
segments.s616-g50: بدین مرغ با من مکن کارزار = prohibition about fighting over the bird. بدین here functions idiomatically as 'with/to this' (classical spelling splits as ب + دین); مرغ is the object 'this bird'; با من 'with me' indicates the other party; مکن is the negative imperative 'do not do'; کارزار means 'battle/war'. The clause forbids engaging in battle with the speaker concerning that bird.
segments.s616-g51: چو بهرام برخاست از خواب خوش = temporal subordinate clause 'when Bahram rose from pleasant sleep'. چو here means 'when'; بهرام is the subject; برخاست is the past action 'rose, got up'; از + خواب خoش = 'from a pleasant sleep' with the adjective خوش following the noun in the usual classical order.
segments.s616-g52: بشد نزد آن باره دست‌کش = main clause reporting that a gauntlet appeared/was presented. بشد is the poetic/past form meaning 'it happened / came to be'; نزد آن باره = 'near/at that occasion/place' (نزد = in the presence of/at); دست‌کش = 'gauntlet' (here: a challenge‑glove). The construction is impersonal: 'a gauntlet came to/was presented at that place.'
segments.s616-g53: که زین برنهد تا به ایوان شود = relative/purpose clause starting with که ('so that/that'). زین (زین) = 'saddle' is the direct object; برنهد is a classical verb form 'he lifted/put on (the saddle)' (commonly used for mounting); تا ... شود = 'until/until he came to/become at the iwan' (تا = until; شود used as past/preterite/auxiliary meaning 'arrive/become'). Grammatically the clause describes the action of mounting and proceeding to the iwan.
segments.s616-g54: کلاهش ز ایوان به کیوان شود = a short, hyperbolic clause describing the hat's movement. کلاهش = 'his hat' (possessive -ش); ز = poetic 'from'; ایوان = location 'the iwan/porch'; به کیوان شود literally 'becomes to Kayvān' — i.e. it flies up to Kayvān (Kayvān = Saturn/the heavens used poetically). The verb شود expresses resulting state/motion ('fell/reached').
segments.s616-g55: چو شاگرد دیدش به بهرام گفت = temporal clause + reporting clause. چو = 'when'; شاگرد = 'the attendant/apprentice'; دیدش = 'saw him' (verb دید + object pronoun -ش); به بهرام = 'to Bahram' (prepositional object of گفت); گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here marking direct speech that follows).
segments.s616-g56: که امروز با من به بد باش جفت = reported imperative/request. که introduces the content of what was said; امروز = 'today'; با من = 'with me' (literally 'be paired/engaged with me'); به بد باش = imperative phrase literally 'be to/beside bad' meaning 'take part in/be matched in ill/evil' (an idiomatic classical turn meaning 'be my match/adversary/companion in misfortune'); جفت = 'mate/companion' used vocatively/nominally to mark the addressee as a partner. Overall: 'Today be my match (in bad fortune) / join me today in hostile/ill action.'
segments.s616-g105: به بازارگان گفت is a simple past clause: به marks the indirect object ('to the merchants'), بازارگان is the plural/collective 'merchants', and گفت is the past tense 'said'. تا زنده‌ای is a temporal subordinate: تا here means 'as long as/while' and زنده‌ای is an elliptical predicate 'you/one are alive' (the copula is omitted in poetic style).
segments.s616-g106: چنان دان is an imperative: چنان 'thus/so' + دان (imperative of 'to know/consider') = 'know/consider thus'. که introduces the content clause that follows. شاگرد را بنده‌ای uses را as a topical/definite marker on شاگرد, and بنده‌ای is a predicative phrase ('is a servant') with the copula omitted (typical of older/poetic Persian).
segments.s616-g107: همان نیز is a resumptive/emphatic phrase ('that same also' or 'likewise'). هر ماهیانی دوبار is a temporal-frequency expression: هر + ماهیانی (here functioning as 'each month' in archaic/poetic usage) and دوبار 'twice'. The word order is characteristic of classical diction.
segments.s616-g108: درم شست is a nominal phrase giving an amount (in older diction the numeral follows or accompanies the noun) — 'sixty dirhams' in context. گنجی بروبر شمار is a verb phrase: گنجی 'treasure' (object/topic), بروبر 'exactly/precisely' (adverb), and شمار here functions as an imperative/verb 'count' (or as a verbal noun used imperatively in poetic compression).
segments.s616-g109: به چیز تو is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'by/with your thing (gift/provision)' where به marks the means. شاگرد مهمان کند is a clause with شاگرد as subject and کند (present/habitual or future) 'makes/causes' — here meaning 'the pupil will host/entertain (a) guest'; مهمان is a predicative/noun complement.
segments.s616-g110: دل مرد آزاده is a genitive/possessive grouping 'the heart of the free/noble man' (دل followed by its modifier). خندان کند is a causative/resultive verbal phrase: خندان 'smiling/cheerful' as predicate adjective + کند 'makes' = 'makes [his] heart glad/causes it to smile'.
segments.s616-g111: به موبد چنین گفت is a straightforward clause 'he said thus to the mobed' (به marks the indirect object, چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'said'). زان پس که is a conjunctive phrase: زان 'from that/therefore' + پس 'after/then' + که introduces the following clause (causal/temporal link). شاه is the subject continued into the next segment.
segments.s616-g112: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when/as'. کار جهان را is the object/phrase 'the affairs (work) of the world' with را marking definiteness/topicality. ندارد نگاه places the negative auxiliary ندارد 'does not have' before نگاه 'attention/gaze' — a poetic order meaning 'does not keep watch/pay attention'.
segments.s616-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بازارگانش is بازارگان (merchant) plus the -ش possessive 'his' = 'his merchants'. فرود آوردید is a past action: فرود (alighting/down) + آوردید (brought/caused) — an older/polite past plural form meaning 'made (him) descend' or 'brought (him) down'. The segment as a whole = 'when his merchants brought him down'.
segments.s616-g10: مر here functions as a dative ('to/for me') in older usage in the line’s syntax (contextually read as marking direction/beneficiary); او را is the object pronoun 'him' with the object marker را. یکی is 'one (of them)' and خوابگه is 'sleeping-place/resting-place'. برگزید is past 'chose/selected'. The clause reads literally '(to/for me) him-OBJ one sleeping-place chose' → 'one chose a sleeping‑place for him.'
segments.s616-g11: همی بود is a habitual/continuous past: همی 'still/continually' + بود 'was'. نالان is a present-participial adjective 'lamenting/moaning'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of', followed by درد شکم 'stomach pain'. The phrase means 'he was still lamenting from stomach pain.'
segments.s616-g12: The clause is SOV-adjacent: به بازارگان داد = 'gave to the merchant' (به marks the beneficiary). لختی درم is a small-amount phrase (a little dirham / a small sum). Classical Persian often places the verb with its indirect object before the direct object phrase here, so the line reads 'to the merchant he gave a small dirham.'
segments.s616-g13: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بد = به + او contracted in older usage + گفت 'said'). لختی here functions as 'a little / for a moment' or 'a little amount' modifying نبید کهن: نبید کهن = 'old wine' (نبید = wine, کهن = old). The segment = 'he said to him, "(give) a little old wine".'
segments.s616-g14: ابا here is an exhortative/negative-root word in older Persian meaning roughly 'do not be reluctant / be willing' or an imperative encouragement preceding the command. مغز بادام = 'almond kernels' (مغز = kernel/core, بادام = almond). بریان بکن is a two-part verb: بریان 'roasted' + بکن (imperative 'do') = 'roast (them)'. Altogether: 'Don't be reluctant — roast the almond kernels.'
segments.s616-g15: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. خانگی مرغ is 'a domestic/home fowl' (خانگی 'domestic', مرغ 'bird/fowl'). باشد is the present/subjunctive copula 'be' (classical subjunctive/optative sense), and رواست is 'it is fitting/permissible' (روا + است contracted). The clause reads 'If it is a domestic fowl, it is fitting.'
segments.s616-g16: کزین = 'from these' (کز + این). آرزوها is the plural 'wishes/desires'. دلم را is 'my heart' (دل + possessive -م) followed by the object/contrast marker را; هواست is هوا 'desire/longing' + ست/است 'is'. Together: 'From these desires, my heart has a longing' / 'my heart longs for these things.'
segments.s616-g1: دگر هفته (literally “another/next week”) places the quantifier/adverb before the noun, a normal Persian order here. تنها is an adverb “alone” modifying the following verbal phrase. به marks direction/purpose “to” and takes نخچیر (“the hunt/game”) as its complement; شد is the past of شدن and here functions as a simple intransitive past ('he went / took himself to' / 'became engaged in'), so the clause means “(on) another week he went alone to the hunt.”
segments.s616-g2: دژم بود is a two-word predication: دژم (adjective) + بود (past of بودن) = “he was sullen/grim.” با introduces instruments/companions (“with”), and the following string ترک + ش + و تیر should be read as a possessed noun (ترکش = ‘his quiver/arrow-case’) plus تیر “arrow(s).” شد at the end again is شدن used as a simple past copula/verb meaning “became/was” or “was thus.”
segments.s616-g3: ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'from' marking cause or source ('because of/from'), so ز خورشید تابنده شد reads 'from the shining sun (lit. the shining sun having been) the plain became warm.' تابنده is an adjective (shining) agreeing in sense with the noun خورشید; شد is the past of شدن meaning 'became.'
segments.s616-g4: سپهبد is the subject (the spahbed / general). ز نخچیر uses ز = 'from' to mark origin ('from the hunt'). برگشت is a past/simple perfect verb 'returned'; نرم is an adverb/adjectival complement 'softly/gently' describing the manner of return.
segments.s616-g5: سوی is a directional preposition 'toward/to'; it governs the following noun phrase کاخ بازارگانی 'the palace of commerce/merchants.' رسید is the past verb 'arrived/reached.' The phrase order is typical classical Persian: direction + place + verb.
segments.s616-g6: به هر سو نگه کرد is literal 'he cast a look toward every direction': به + هر سو = 'toward every side', نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (did) = 'looked'. The second clause و کس را ندید uses را as the definite-object marker and ندید (past of دیدن) negated by context to mean 'he saw no one' — کس here functions with the negative to mean 'no one.'
segments.s616-g7: ببازارگان is the agent phrase 'the merchants' (the initial b- is a prepositional/poetic form meaning 'by' or simply a prefixed marker here). گفت is the speech verb 'said' (the supplied senses list was lexical rather than inflectional; here it functions as the past-tense verb 'said'). ما را is the object 'to us' (ما = we/us, را marks the object), and سپنج is a noun 'sponge' (used metaphorically here).
segments.s616-g8: توان داد is a light/verbal sequence: توان can be read as the ability/power or an auxiliary 'can', and داد is the past of دادن 'gave' — together here meaning roughly 'was able to give / gave.' کز is the poetic contraction of که + از meaning 'that/from' or simply 'that,' introducing a subordinate clause. ما is 'us', نبینی is negative subjunctive/present of دیدن 'to see' with second-person singular form (you), so کز ما نبینی تو رنج = 'so that you would not see our suffering.' رنج is the object 'suffering/distress.'
segments.s616-g33: پشیمان شد is a two-word predicate: پشیمان (regretful) + شد (became) = 'became regretful'. از marks the cause ('because of' / 'on account of') and introduces گفت (here a noun meaning 'the saying' or 'utterance') followed by the content نان بخورد ('that he should eat bread'); بخورد is the present/subjunctive form of خوردن used to report the content of the utterance ('to eat' / 'let him eat').
segments.s616-g34: برو here means 'he went/left' in narrative (not an imperative); نیز = 'also/likewise'. یاد گذشته is the noun phrase 'memory/remembrance of the past', and نکرد is the negative past of کردن 'to do' forming 'did not remember' (literally 'did not do remembrance').
segments.s616-g35: چو ... آمد is a temporal clause: چو = 'when' and آمد = 'came'. هنگامه here means 'the moment/occasion' (the time for something), خوابش = 'his sleep' (خواب + possessive suffix -ش). بخفت is an older past form meaning 'he lay down / slept', placed as the main verb of the clause.
segments.s616-g36: به بازارگان = 'to/for the merchants' (به + indirect object). نیز = 'also'; چیزی (indefinite object) + نگفت (negative past of گفتن) = 'said nothing' (literally 'did not say anything'). Word order is prepositional object → adverb → object → verb.
segments.s616-g37: ز (poetic 'from') introduces the source 'دریای جوشان' = 'the seething/boiling sea' (دریای + جوشان). چو = 'when' introduces the temporal clause; خور ('the sun') is the subject and بردمید is a past verb meaning 'took away / carried off' or more generally 'rose/tore forth' — here part of the temporal image of the sun appearing from the sea.
segments.s616-g38: شد is the past auxiliary meaning 'became / happened' introducing the resulting state. آن چادر قیرگون is the noun phrase 'that pitch-black tent' (آن = 'that', چادر = 'tent', قیرگون = 'pitch-black'), and ناپدید is the predicate 'vanished' / 'became unseen'.
segments.s616-g39: همی is an adverb expressing continuous or habitual action ('kept on' / 'still'), placed before the verb گفت ('said'). پرمایه بازارگان is the subject noun phrase 'the substantial / well-off merchant' (پرمایه modifying بازارگان).
segments.s616-g40: به شاگرد = 'to the apprentice' (به + indirect object). کای is a vocative particle 'O', so کای مرد ناکاردان is direct address: 'O man, incompetent one' (مرد + adjective ناکاردان). The whole phrase marks what the merchant was addressing to his apprentice.
segments.s616-g25: نیاوردی is a second-person singular past negative ('you did not bring'). و simply links the clauses ('and'). داده بودم is a compound past (pluperfect) formed from the past participle داده + auxiliary بودم meaning 'I had given'; درم is the direct object 'a dirham' placed after the verbal phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s616-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason ('for/because'); نالنده بودم is a predication made of the present-participle/noun نالنده + past auxiliary بودم meaning 'I was moaning/complaining'; ز درد شکم is a poetic prepositional phrase ('from the pain of the stomach') with ز = 'from' + درد شکم = 'stomach pain'.
segments.s616-g27: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact clause 'thus gave an answer' (چنین = thus, داد = gave, پاسخ = answer). که‌ای begins the reported/direct address: the particle که introduces the speech and ای (vocative) addresses the addressee; بی‌خرد is the vocative adjective 'O fool/unwise one.'
segments.s616-g28: نداری خرد is a simple predication 'you have no wisdom' where نداری (here = 'you lack') is a negative-present verb and خرد is the noun 'wisdom'. کو is an interrogative/relative 'where is…' that launches the following phrase; روان پرورد is a compact relative idea 'the spirit/soul that nurtured (or nurtures)' with پرورد functioning as a formative of 'to nurture' (a verbal adjective/agent idea).
segments.s616-g29: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آوردم is first-person past 'I brought'; این مرغ بریان گرم is the noun phrase 'this bird, roasted and hot' where بریان and گرم are adjectival modifiers describing مرغ. The clause is a temporal subordinate clause setting the scene.
segments.s616-g30: فزون خواستن is a verbal-nominal phrase 'wanting more' (فزون = more/excess, خواستن = to want). نیست is the negative copula 'is not', and آیین و شرم ('custom and shame/modesty') form the predicate: literally 'Wanting more is not custom nor modesty' — i.e. it is not in keeping with custom or modesty to desire more.
segments.s616-g31: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; بشنید is third-person past 'he heard' and بهرام is the subject 'Bahram'. ز + و here represent the poetic contraction of از او ('from him'); این سخن is 'this speech.' The whole segment means 'When Bahram heard this speech from him…'.
segments.s616-g32: بشد is an impersonal/past resultive 'it happened/became' (here 'arose'); آرزوی is the abstract noun 'a desire/longing' governing the following genitive phrase; نبید کهن is 'old (or aged) wine' (نبید = wine, کهن = old/ancient). Together: 'a longing for old wine arose.'
segments.s616-g89: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing a subordinate clause. مرغی (object) + خریدی (2nd-person singular past of خریدن) = 'you bought a little bird'; the postposed phrase فزون از بها literally 'greater than the price' modifies the purchase, i.e. 'paid more than it was worth.' Word order is typical older Persian: object before the verb, then the modifying phrase.
segments.s616-g90: نهادی here is the past of نهادن 'you put / you placed' (2nd-person sing. past). م + را together function as the object pronoun 'mara' = 'me' (the tokenization split it). در دم is a locative/poetic phrase 'in the breath / mouth / moment' and is followed by اژدها 'of the dragon' — together 'into the dragon's breath/mouth'.
segments.s616-g91: بگفت is an honorific/pastform 'he said' (classical be‑ + گفت). این is the demonstrative 'this' as object, followed by the dative به بازارگان 'to the merchants'. و برفت links with a classical past 'and he went off / departed' (برفت = رفت + classical prefix).
segments.s616-g92: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. گاه شاهی here means 'the royal place / presence' (گاه used poetically as 'place/seat of the king'); خرامید is the past 'he strode/sauntered proudly'. تفت is a terse poetic element whose literal senses ('to scorch/roast') do not fit directly and acts as an emphatic or descriptive particle here; its exact usage is archaic/poetic.
segments.s616-g93: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as'. خورشید (literally 'the sun', used here as a bright/majestic image) is the subject. بر تخت (on the throne) precedes the verb بنمود (classical past 'showed/placed'), and تاج is the object 'the crown' — so: 'When the sun placed/showed the crown on the throne.'
segments.s616-g94: جهانبان is a title/name (here the person who takes his seat); نشست is the past 'sat' or 'took his seat'. از بر تخت عاج is an archaic locative phrase meaning 'upon the ivory throne' (عاج modifying تخت = 'ivory throne'); the preposition combination از بر is characteristic of classical Persian locative expressions.
segments.s616-g95: بفرمود is an honorific past 'he ordered/commanded'. خسرو is the subject 'the king (Khosrow)'. به سالار بار is a dative phrase 'to the chief of the baggage/quartermaster' (سالار = chief, بار = load/baggage — a court official in charge of stores).
segments.s616-g96: که introduces the reported command 'that...'. بازارگان را is the definite object 'the merchants' (را marks the object). کند is the classical present/subjunctive 'he should do / let him do' used to render an indirect command. خواستار here functions as the predicate meaning 'to have (someone) requested/called' — together 'that he have the merchants called' (i.e. summon them).
segments.s616-g81: شما (personal pronoun) comes before the verb as the 2nd-person plural/formal subject. می‌گسارید is a present-tense verb with the habitual/continuous prefix می‑ and 2pl ending ‑ید; here it means 'you scatter/squander'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. مستان is a predicative plural noun 'drunkards', and شوید is the verb 'become' in the present/subjunctive form (2pl) used to say 'become X'.
segments.s616-g82: مجنبید is a negative imperative/jussive for 2pl ('do not move' / 'do not stir'). تا introduces a subordinate clause with temporal/result sense 'until'. می‌پرستان is a noun phrase 'wine-drinkers/wine-worshippers' and شوید again is the 2pl present/subjunctive of 'become' so the clause means 'until you become wine-drinkers'.
segments.s616-g83: بمالید here is a past (3rd-person) or classical narrative form 'he rubbed / he anointed' (the b‑ prefix occurs in older usage). پس is an adverb 'then'. باره را uses را as the definite object marker and باره (here) means 'load' or 'bundle'. زین نهاد places the locative/goal زین 'on the saddle' before the verb نهاد, which is the past of نهادن 'to place' — 'he placed (it) on the saddle.'
segments.s616-g84: سوی گلشن آمد is a straightforward classical order: سوی 'toward' + گلشن 'the garden' + آمد 'he came'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and introduces an ablative cause: می‌گشته is the verbal form of گشتن (here meaning 'become' / 'be made' — i.e. intoxicated), and شاد is the predicative adjective 'merry'; together 'merry from wine' (made merry by wine).
segments.s616-g85: به بازارگان گفت is 'he said to the merchants' (به + indirect object + گفت — past 3rd-person of گفتن). چندین is an adverb/adjective 'many / several'. مکوش is the negative imperative/jussive 'do not try / do not strive' (classical negative-command form). The clause therefore records his addressed warning to the merchants.
segments.s616-g86: از introduces a causal/ablative phrase here ('on account of' / 'because of'). افزونی is a noun 'increase, abundance'. این مرد ارزان‌فروش is the noun phrase 'this man, a cheap-seller' (ارزان‌فروش = seller of cheap goods); together the phrase explains the reason: 'because of the abundance/increase of this cheap seller.'
segments.s616-g87: به دانگی is a prepositional/measure phrase 'for a dangi' (a small unit/portion — here a small price). مرا is the object 'me' (written as م + را in the provided segmentation); دوش is an adverb 'last night'. بفروختی is the 2nd-person past 'you sold'. Word order in classical Persian allows the oblique/time phrases before the verb: 'For a dangi you sold me last night.'
segments.s616-g88: همی is an adverb intensifier 'still/indeed' (often used to emphasize the action). چشم شاگرد را places the object 'the pupil's/apprentice's eye' (شاگرد = apprentice/pupil) with the object marker را. دوختی is 2nd-person past of دوختن 'you sewed/you fixed' used here metaphorically 'you fixed/stared (one's eye upon)' — i.e. 'you fixed the apprentice's gaze.'
segments.s616-g41: A compact nominal clause giving the item and its (lack of) price. مرغ کارزش = "the bird's value/price" (کارزش used poetically for the sum or worth). نبد is the older negative past of بودن ("was not"). مران is an irregular/archaic token here (written together) — not a proper-name reading in context but an old or contracted element; the clause as a whole = "the bird's value was not one dirham."
segments.s616-g42: Two coordinated verbal pieces: خریدی is 2nd person past "you bought"; به افزون literally "for an excess/more" = "for more (than it was worth)"; و کردی ستم = "and you did/committed injustice" (کردی = you did, ستم = wrongdoing/injustice). Word order is subject (implied you) + verb + prepositional/adv phrase + coordination.
segments.s616-g43: A conditional protasis introduced by گر (= if/had). ارزان خریدی is "you had bought cheaply" (خریدی = 2nd person past). ابا این سوار in the manuscript-form is a contracted/poetic variant of با این سوار (= "with this rider"); here it marks accompaniment (note: the printed lemma list gives unrelated senses for ابا, so this is to be read as the preposition با).
segments.s616-g44: Apodosis built around the impersonal/experiential sense of بودن. نبودی is an optative/conditional past negative ("would not have been"); م + را here form the clitic مرا = "for me" (split in the word list); تیره شب کارزار = "a dark/ominous night of battle" (adjective تیره precedes شب). Whole clause = "there would not have been for me a dark night of battle."
segments.s616-g45: A simple transitive past: خریدی = "you bought." مر (here a reduced form written separately in the word list) + او را = مرا او را → "him from me" (مر = me/to me, او را = him as definite object). به دانگی پنیر = a prepositional phrase "for a dangi (a small portion/share) of cheese" specifying the (meagre) price.
segments.s616-g46: Complaint about what was received afterwards. بدی here functions as a verbal/performative past addressed to the addressee (poetic/archaic form, contextually 'you gave' or 'you treated [me]'), followed by با من = "to/with me," امروز = "today," چون آب و شیر = a simile "like water and milk" (i.e. only humble refreshments). The verb form is archaic/contracted in the text; sense must be inferred from context.
segments.s616-g47: Reported speech opener: بدو گفت = "he said to him" (بدو = to him; گفت = said — printed lemma senses are of 'speech' as noun, so here it's the verb 'said'). اگر این نه کار منست = a conditional/hypothetical clause: اگر = if, این = this, نه = not (negation), کار منست = "is my affair/concern" (منست an older adjectival/copular form meaning 'belongs/appropriate to me').
segments.s616-g48: Imperative/performative follow-up: چنان دان = "consider/know it so" (چنان = thus/so, دان = imperative or jussive of دانستن used as 'consider'). که introduces the content: مرغ از شمار منست = "that the bird is from my number/is counted among mine" (از شمار = 'from the count/of the tally'). The clause asks the addressee to treat the bird as belonging to the speaker.

sentences.s616-s57: How would he know which people are which; how could he distinguish the world from the moon?
sentences.s616-s33: Now let us bring warm wishes, and also several kinds of tender dishes.
sentences.s616-s34: He said this, and then went to the bazaar; to a changed tune he became a buyer.
sentences.s616-s35: He took sugar, almonds, poultry, and lamb to complete the banquet's arrangement.
sentences.s616-s36: He took wine, saffron, musk, and rosewater, and turned toward the house with a hurried heart.
sentences.s616-s29: The king rose and sat upon his throne; astonishment at his fate remained.
sentences.s616-s30: The young man went and brought two hundred testicles. He said to the master, "O honored one, it is the moon."
sentences.s616-s31: One roasted fowl with warm bread; bring aged wine and soft almonds.
sentences.s616-s32: He came near Bahram and said, "O rider! Have you long been a seeker of testicles?"
sentences.s616-s9: The merchant did not bring what he had said; there was no almond kernel hidden inside it.
sentences.s616-s10: When it grew dark, the host went away softly and (then) brought a hot roasted fowl.
sentences.s616-s11: He set the banquet in order and brought it before Bahram; he said to the merchant, “(Be) Bahram‑like.”
sentences.s616-s12: For I wished for from you old wine; I had adorned my speech with entreaty.
sentences.s616-s49: They brought the pupil together with him; one of them was cheerful and the other sullen.
sentences.s616-s50: When the pupil and the master went off quickly, they came before the Shahanshah of Iran like smoke.
sentences.s616-s51: When (the king) saw the pupil, he struck/played for him and, joyful, set him before the chiefs.
sentences.s616-s52: They brought a beauty near him; when she became like the moon, his soul grew dark.
sentences.s616-s37: He brought a table with delightful cooked dishes; the young man was of good character and pure‑hearted.
sentences.s616-s38: When the bread had been eaten, he used to take the full cup; first of all he handed it to Bahram Khosrow.
sentences.s616-s39: Thus, until they became merry and joyful; because of the little morsels they continually went to the cup.
sentences.s616-s40: Thus the king said to the host that Bahram desired us.
sentences.s616-s25: Be my guest with this rider; do not make war with me over this bird.
sentences.s616-s26: When Bahram rose from a pleasant sleep, a gauntlet was brought to that place.
sentences.s616-s27: that he mounted his saddle until he reached the iwan; his cap flew from the iwan up to Kayvān (the sky).
sentences.s616-s28: When the attendant saw him, he said to Bahram: 'Be my match in misfortune today.'
sentences.s616-s53: He said to the merchants: "As long as you live, know this — consider the pupil a servant."
sentences.s616-s54: Likewise, twice every month: count the treasure exactly — sixty dirhams.
sentences.s616-s55: With your provision the pupil will entertain a guest, and it will make the free man's heart smile.
sentences.s616-s56: He said this to the mobed: for afterwards, when the king does not keep watch over the affairs of the world...
sentences.s616-s5: When his merchants brought him down, one of them chose a place to sleep for him.
sentences.s616-s6: He continued lamenting from a stomach pain; to the merchant he gave a small dirham.
sentences.s616-s7: He said to him, 'Give a little old wine; do roast the almond kernels.'
sentences.s616-s8: If it is a domestic bird it is fitting; from these wishes my heart has a longing.
sentences.s616-s1: The following week he went alone to the hunt; he grew sullen and was armed with his quiver and arrows.
sentences.s616-s2: From the shining sun the plain became warm; the spahbed returned gently from the hunt.
sentences.s616-s3: He reached the merchants' palace, looked in every direction, and saw no one.
sentences.s616-s4: The merchants said, 'Give us a sponge' — (or:) 'He could grant us a sponge so that you would not see our suffering.'
sentences.s616-s17: He regretted his own words 'let him eat bread'; he went away and likewise did not remember the past.
sentences.s616-s18: When the time for sleep came he lay down; he also said nothing to the merchants.
sentences.s616-s19: When the sun rose from the seething sea, that pitch-black tent disappeared.
sentences.s616-s20: The wealthy merchant kept saying to the apprentice, 'O man, you incompetent one.'
sentences.s616-s13: You did not bring (it), and I had given a dirham, for I was moaning from stomach pain.
sentences.s616-s14: Thus he answered: 'O foolish one, you have no wisdom — where is the spirit that nurtures it?'
sentences.s616-s15: When I brought this roasted, hot bird, it is not proper nor modest to ask for more.
sentences.s616-s16: When Bahram heard this speech from him, a longing for old wine arose.
sentences.s616-s45: That you bought a little bird for more than its price and put me into the dragon's breath.
sentences.s616-s46: He said this to the merchants and departed; he strode proudly toward the king's presence.
sentences.s616-s47: When the sun set the crown upon the throne, Jahānban sat upon the ivory throne.
sentences.s616-s48: The king commanded the chief of the baggage that the merchants be summoned.
sentences.s616-s41: You scatter (things) and become drunkards; do not move until you become wine-drinkers.
sentences.s616-s42: He rubbed (it), then set the load on the saddle; toward the garden he came, made merry by wine.
sentences.s616-s43: He said to the merchants, 'Do not strive so much on account of the increasing number/abundance of this cheap-seller.'
sentences.s616-s44: You sold me last night for a dangi; you fixed the pupil's eye (i.e. fixed the apprentice's gaze).
sentences.s616-s21: The bird's value was not one dirham; you bought it for more and committed an injustice.
sentences.s616-s22: Had you bought (him) cheaply with this rider, I would not have had a dark night of battle.
sentences.s616-s23: You bought him from me for the price of a piece of cheese; today you gave me nothing but water and milk.
sentences.s616-s24: He said to him, "If this is not my business, then consider it so that the bird is counted among mine."
